item.title: Future Generations
item.description:
The Wellbeing of Future Generations Act 2015 for Wales

Wellbeing of Future Generations Act 7 goals Globally responsible Wales Prosperous Wales Resilient Wales Healthier Wales More equal Wales Wales of cohesive communities Wales of vibrant culture and thriving Welsh language 5 ways of working The long term - Avoiding short-termism and considering how decisions will impact on the well-being of future as well as current generations. http://localhost:1313/future-generations/

item.title: How to transform school meals? Beans is how!
item.description:
Very inspirational blog post about improving UK school meals. It is a lot of work but worth it

Ivy is a fantastic wildlife plant but do watch out where it’s growing

item.title: Tools
item.description:
These are my most favourite tools, really here for my own reference!

Most used & favourite Felco 2 secateurs with Housolution polyurethane sheath Silky F180 folding saw, with replaceable blade Niwaki hori-hori knife & canvas sheath Showa 350R 10/XL gloves Spear & Jackson solid socket tree spade Wishlist Silky Zubat 330 with belt clip scabbard Niwaki GR Pro secateurs — I like Felco secateurs as they’re easy to take apart and maintain, but I don’t like the latch by the blades, much prefer a latch at the bottom of the handles like on the Niwaki secateurs. http://localhost:1313/tools/

item.title: Tools of the Trade

item.title: Radical Horticulture Manifesto
item.description:
Some thoughts and musings on a (not really so)

Every garden can be a #WildlifeGarden Use #NativePlants where possible Create habitat where you can Grow #WildFlowers & perennial crops for humans Extend garden principles of mutual aid into the commons Common land Accessible, affordable Create edible ecosystems Prerequisites #Organic #PeatFree #PlantBased Local & recycled materials Minimal energy Minimal plastic Questions Who owns the land? http://localhost:1313/blog/230917-radical-horticulture-manifesto/

item.title: Wildlife Gardening Forum evening

item.title: Protection
item.description:
Plants need protection to survive, particularly in their early stages

Protect & thrive! Protection — from wind, frost, sun & predators Wind Windbreak hedge Nurse tree Dead hedge Wind & cold Potting shed Greenhouse Polytunnel Cold frames Heated propagation bed Protect some young plants from direct sun Protect young plants from drying out Protect from weed competition Protect from predators Note: will be updating this page with examples & photos http://localhost:1313/protection/

item.title: Are you ready?
item.description:
The climate and ecological emergency is happening. A forest garden is fundamentally sustainable, reducing carbon emissions & increasing biodiversity, whilst providing a resilient food system in case of emergency.

The Covid-19 coronavirus crisis shines a light on how ill-prepared and unequal our society has become. http://localhost:1313/blog/are-you-ready/

Summary of how to create a nutrient budget for your forest garden

item.title: Life as the measure
item.description:
Gross Domestic Product is a pretty useless measure of a country’s prosperity, it’s time to factor in the true cost of economic activity by using life as the measure

Don’t know much about ecology, don’t know much economics either but what I do know is what a wonderful world it would be, if we used life as the measure, rather than human derived financial value (AKA money). http://localhost:1313/blog/life-as-the-measure/

item.title: Estimate
item.description:
Approx time & money estimates for different forest garden jobs, per 100m²

Estimate per 100m² Assume clearing grass land 1 tonne bark or wood chip mulch, £35 per tonne Approx 400 plants (3-5 per m²) Approx 250g White Mustard seed temp ground cover plant Ground preparation Sheet mulch Sheet mulch per 100m² (2m x 50m) £40 Metal pegs x50 £10 Job Time Mow 3 hr Lay sheet mulch 3 hr Leave 2-3 mth Lift sheet mulch 3 hr Lay bark 6 hr Plant ground cover 4 hr Sow temp cover 1 hr Total 20 hr Scraping Mini-digger 1 day hire £100 Digger drive 1 day £100 Diesel £20 Only cost effective for half hectare site, ⅓ of which scraped ie 1500 m². http://localhost:1313/estimate/

item.title: Nurse tree
item.description:
A nurse tree is a temporary shelter plant for a newly planted tree

A nurse tree is a fast-growing tree or shrub planted a couple of metres away from a newly planted specimen tree, in the windward direction, normally grown in groups of 2 or 3. http://localhost:1313/nurse-tree/

item.description: Forest garden for English village property http://localhost:1313/portfolio/hedgerows/

item.title: Wildflower
item.description: Resources for starting out with UK native wild flowers. Botanical Society of Britain & Ireland Get Started For ID BSBI’s Botanical Keys Makaques FloralImages photo ID Wildflowers of Britain and Ireland Facebook group For ecology BSBI’s Plant Atlas For garden Plantlife’s Wild Flowers To Grow In The Garden For garden RHS Plant Finder, try with Native Filter For “useful” plants Plants For A Future Books Harrap’s Wild Flowers Collin’s Flower Guide The Wildflower Key Suppliers Barry Stewart at Celtic Wild Flowers Lindsey Jones at The Wildflower Nursery Matt Smith at British Wild Flower Plants Thank you Moira O’Donnell and Martin Allen 🙂 http://localhost:1313/wildflower/

item.title: The idea of a modular forest garden
item.description:
Modular design can make designed meadow planting easier but can it do the same for a forest garden?

I’m fascinated by Benjamin Vogt’s idea of using modular design for a native garden meadow. http://localhost:1313/blog/modular-forest-garden/

My notes from fantastic talk ‘Wild Orchids of Wales’ by Sue Parker, Publicity Officer at Hardy Orchid Society

item.title: Hostas
item.description:
Many hostas are used as vegetables, list from Martin Crawford’s ‘Creating a Forest Garden’

Hosta crispula 0.6m x 1m  Hosta longipes 0.3m x 1m http://localhost:1313/hostas/

item.title: Chop ‘n’ drop
item.description:
Plant comfrey by your fruit trees, then chop the leaves once or twice a year and mulch in-situ. This is the chop ‘n’ drop comfrey mulch technique, no composting required.

Comfrey is a garden workhorse. As a mineral accumulator, it’s rich in potassium and is used to make a liquid compost. http://localhost:1313/chop-n-drop/

item.title: Recommended
item.description:
Recommended books, suppliers, websites and podcasts. Saving you time and enlarging your brain 🧠

Books Creating a Forest Garden by Martin Crawford A New Garden Ethic by Benjamin Vogt, the morality of gardening with natives The Wild Garden, by Violet Stevenson, 1985 but pretty much the only book covering the use of UK native plants Around the world in 80 plants by Stephen Barstow, a global romp around edible plants The Garden of Equal Delights by Anni Kelsey, forest gardening from the gardener’s perspective, delightful and intriguing Suppliers Backyard Larder, Alison Tindale Incredible Veg, Mandy Barber Agroforestry Research Trust, Martin Crawford Cool Temperate, Phil Corbett Future Forests, for hedging Websites Plants For A Future brilliant database of useful plants, essential Database of Insects & their Food intoxifying amount of info about which plants are hosts for which insects orangepippin. http://localhost:1313/recommended/

item.title: Top 10 perennial veg
item.description:
Inspired by Backyard Larder, Incredible Vegetables and Martin Crawford, here’s my ‘it could change at any moment’ top 10 perennial veg

Caucasian Spinach Day Lillies Welsh Onion Garden Sorrel Good King Henry Perennial Kale Perennial Leek Siberian Purslane Turkish Rocket Wild Garlic Big thank you to Alison Tindale at Backyard Larder for some great plants, profiles and photos, to Martin Crawford for writing Creating a Forest Garden, to Mandy Barber at Incredible Vegetables for the inspiration, and to Stephen Barstow for his unrelenting exploration of all things edimental. http://localhost:1313/top-10-perennial-veg/

item.title: My take on Planting in a Post-wild World
item.description: My entry into gardening was via the forest garden, so I find the emphasis on the ornamental in mainstream gardening quite perplexing, particularly after reading Benjamin Vogt’s A New Garden Ethic. http://localhost:1313/blog/planting-post-wild-world/

item.title: Hoverfly & wild pollinators research at Botanic Garden Wales
item.description:
Abigail Lowe at the Botanic Garden Wales is researching which are the most beneficial flowers to plant for hoverflies and other wild pollinators

On a recent visit to Botanic Garden Wales, I met up with Abigail Lowe, a PhD student with the Garden’s Science Team who is continuing the critical research into which flowers pollinators prefer, focussing on the role of hoverflies. http://localhost:1313/blog/hoverfly-wild-pollinators-botanic-garden-wales/

item.title: The Japanese Forest Garden
item.description:
My client in Poland wants a Japanese-style garden incorporated into the forest garden, so I made a special trip to meet the staff responsible for the Japanese Garden at Botanic Garden Wales

At first glance, the idea of a Japanese Garden is at odds with a forest garden: clipped cloud hedges, carefully trained trees, layer upon layer of symbolism and meaning. http://localhost:1313/blog/japanese-forest-garden/

item.title: This is a local garden, for local species
item.description: Humans are strange creatures, capable of abstracting their minds unfathomable distances from their situation. http://localhost:1313/blog/local-garden-local-species/

item.title: Creating a forest garden plan with a satellite background image
item.description:
As part of my online forest garden course, I’ve been looking at how to use a screenshot of a satellite photo as the basis of a forest garden plan. If you’re comfortable with computers, the process is relatively straightforward.

Using a CAD plan, it’s easy to move trees around to try out different ideas. http://localhost:1313/blog/creating-forest-garden-plan-satellite/

item.title: Green Room
item.description: Ornamental forest garden for Victorian London terrace http://localhost:1313/portfolio/greenroom/

item.title: Pretty forest garden pictures
item.description:
Despite my ambivalence toward aesthetics, a pretty picture can connect a wildlife forest garden with a wider audience

Whilst designing a small wildlife forest garden for some clients in London, I was searching for information about the Teasel on the RHS plant finder. http://localhost:1313/blog/pretty-forest-garden-pictures/

item.title: Recording Online Course
item.description: I’m in the middle of writing the slideshow script for the forest garden online course, and I thought somebody might be interested in my recording setup, particularly if you’d like to create a course yourself. http://localhost:1313/blog/recording-online-course/

item.title: The meaning of Latin plant names
item.description:
Handy reference for finding out what scientific plant names mean, via The Seed Site

The Seed Site has a great list of the meaning of Latin plant names. http://localhost:1313/blog/meaning-latin-plant-names/

item.title: State of Nature report 2019
item.description:
Links to the State of Nature report 2019 from the British Trust for Ornithology @_BTO. Because the mainstream media really doesn’t seem that interested.

I gave a talk about The Smaller Forest Garden the other day, and only 3 people out of the two dozen strong audience had heard of the State of Nature report 2019 published October 2019. http://localhost:1313/blog/state-nature-report-2019/

item.title: Inter-disciplinary forest garden
item.description:
A forest garden is a niche that falls between “traditional” gardening disciplines

In my previous life as web designer, I attended the leftfield tech conference dConstruct back in 2012. http://localhost:1313/blog/inter-disciplinary-forest-garden/

item.title: One screenshot to change the way you think about native plants
item.description:
Do you know how many bugs the Common Oak supports? This screenshot from the ‘Database of Insects and their Food Plants’ took my breath away

The Database of Insects and their Food Plants is a fantastic resource created by the Biological Records Centre that lists native plants and which inverterbrates they are hosts for. http://localhost:1313/blog/screenshot-think-native-plants/

item.title: If we don’t change direction, we’ll end up where we’re going
item.description:
I gave a talk to Llangwm Gardening Club about The Smaller Forest Garden. I digressed onto Native Plants and the Climate Emergency. Oops.

My talk on The Smaller Forest Garden for Llangwm Gardening Club seemed to go down well (slides here: forestgarden. http://localhost:1313/blog/end-up-going/

item.title: My favourite place
item.description:
I like being in forest gardens & living places. You can give it a name (biophilia) if you like. But I just like being there.

I finished weeding in the morning. The geranium has taken well in most places, pungent where I tread too close. http://localhost:1313/blog/my-favourite-place/

item.title: Overwhelmed by Vegetables
item.description:
I’m in transition to perennial vegetables but whatever the crop, it is easy to be overwhelmed by vegetables!

First off, here’s my simple, pot-bound, bung-it-in-together tomato purée recipe. TL;DR don’t fret, chuck it in a pot. http://localhost:1313/blog/overwhelmed-by-vegetables/

item.title: Overview of my forest garden design process
item.description:
A bird’s eye view of my approach to designing a forest garden, in the form of a slideshow and screencast

I’ve been asking friends and colleagues for feedback about what would make a good online course for forest gardening. http://localhost:1313/blog/overview-forest-garden-design-process/

item.title: Bruce Slark Forest Garden Talk
item.description:
Bruce Slark has been growing a forest garden for nigh on 20 years and his talk at Moylgrove Hall was full of tasty nuggets

Bruce’s talk on his well-established forest garden at Moylgrove Hall was very interesting, particularly because it is well-established. http://localhost:1313/blog/bruce-slark-forest-garden-talk/

item.title: More or less politics
item.description:
Time to redirect my politically charged radical sentiments elsewhere methinks

You might have noticed a bit of heat being generated about Brexit recently. http://localhost:1313/blog/more-less-politics/

item.title: Wildlife: the power of a name
item.description:
Gardening for others is so much more rewarding, particularly when you know their names

Benjamin Vogt’s @BRVogt article Native Plants, Racism, & the Colonizing Nature of Garden Language is thought provoking stuff. http://localhost:1313/blog/wildlife-power-name/

item.title: Pivot
item.description:
I’m switching my work to concentrate on wildlife and forest garden design, with a view to an online course next year.

I gambled on making a living from running forest garden tours and workshops, using the Airbnb network to get passing holiday trade. http://localhost:1313/blog/pivot/

item.title: Hawthorn all the way
item.description:
Creating wildlife habitat in a farm garden

I’m very lucky to be working on a large farm garden in Pembrokeshire. http://localhost:1313/blog/hawthorn-all-the-way/

item.title: Peat Free Nurseries
item.description:
Does your nursery use peat-free compost? A list of nurseries that do, compiled by Nic Wilson 🙏

Using peat-based compost contributes to #ClimateBreakdown and wildlife habitat loss. Best thing you can do is use a peat-free compost (such as Sylvagrow, FertileFibre, Dalefoot etc) and buy your plants from a nursery that use peat-free compost. http://localhost:1313/blog/peat-free-nurseries/

item.title: PeatFree Climate Emergency
item.description:
Using peat-based compost contributes to the destruction of wildlife habitats and the release of vast amounts of greenhouse gases. Profit, convenience & ignorance; it is the story of Climate Breakdown.

The peat extraction industry is a microcosm of the wider Climate Emergency. http://localhost:1313/blog/peatfree-climate-emergency/

item.title: Livestream
item.description: The monthly structured livestream is the first Wednesday of the month on my YouTube channel youtube. http://localhost:1313/livestream/

item.title: Rhydgaled
item.description: One acre forest garden for Welsh cottage http://localhost:1313/portfolio/rhydgaled/

item.title: My advice for a forest garden? Flowers and annual veg
item.description:
Every garden is dynamic, changing over time. When you’re starting out with a forest garden, pollinator friendly flowers and annual veg can be a good place to start.

I have the occasional taker for my forest garden tour and sharing what I know with others is something I really enjoy. http://localhost:1313/blog/flowers-annual-veg/

item.title: Let go control
item.description:
Other work in my life has meant a slow down in the forest garden but the trees still grow and the wildlife prospers. Letting go can be a hard but necessary lesson.

The all-summer-rolling forest garden tours and workshops via Airbnb were always a bit of a gamble. http://localhost:1313/blog/let-go-control/

item.title: Biological recording workshop
item.description:
Clare Flynn ran a fantastic wildlife recording workshop at The People’s Orchard in St Dogmaels, here are my notes to my future self

West Wales Biodiversity Information Centre (WWBIC), made up of Pembrokeshire, Ceredigion & Carmarthen. http://localhost:1313/blog/biological-recording-workshop/

item.title: Wild Food Corridors
item.description:
Involving people in the creation of wildlife habitat in public spaces whilst simultaneously providing a harvest is a way to focus minds on our existential environmental threats

We need to protect and restore our wildlife whilst dramatically reducing our Greenhouse Gas emissions, which means sustainable food production on a global scale, something that can only happen with public awareness, understanding and support. http://localhost:1313/blog/wild-food-corridors/

item.title: Clay lined pond
item.description:
Nick Balmer wrote a fantastic summary of creating a clay-lined pond in the closed Facebook Forest Garden UK group, reproduced here with his kind permission 🙂

I’m creating yet another wildlife pond, this time in the Ornamental Forest Garden. http://localhost:1313/clay-lined-pond/

item.title: Wildlife features
item.description:
Checklist of invaluable features for your wildlife friendly garden

A list of features that benefit wildlife in any garden. http://localhost:1313/wildlife-features/

item.title: Wildlife garden checklist
item.description:
Inspired by Malcolm Berry’s talk, here’s a simple checklist of invaluable features for your wildlife-friendly garden

Inspired by Malcolm Berry’s wildlife gardening talk to Drefach Gardening Club, here is a simple list of features that benefit wildlife in any garden. http://localhost:1313/blog/wildlife-garden-checklist/

item.title: Designing wilderness well
item.description:
If you create any wildlife friendly space, a forest garden included, care is required to design it well, so as to maximise the benefits.

@BotanicalMartin’s recent tweet resonated deeply with me: I think this will be a blueprint for many of our urban/suburban green spaces in the future - species-rich grassland (irregularly-cut) with scrub (irregularly coppiced), bramble, bare areas, open areas, well-trodden paths. http://localhost:1313/blog/designing-wilderness-well/

item.title: Forest garden ground cover preparation
item.description:
The “traditional” way of clearing the ground is to use sheet mulch. An alternative and possibly less wasteful way is to scrape the ground with a mini-digger.

In the original forest garden, I used vast amounts of woven plastic sheet mulch to clear the grass, before laying down bark mulch and planting perennial ground cover (a process I’ve described previously). http://localhost:1313/blog/forest-garden-ground-cover-prep/

item.title: Forest garden CAD photo
item.description:
Using a satellite photo as the basis for your forest garden plan is a good and quick way of creating an approximate plan without having to measure out in the field.

I discovered how to easily scale a satellite photo in QCAD to create a #ForestGarden plan. http://localhost:1313/blog/forest-garden-cad-photo/

item.title: Community Food Forest
item.description:
A simple checklist for anyone interested in setting up a Community Food Forest (AKA Community Forest Garden)

Someone on the Forest Garden UK group on Facebook is setting up a Community Food Forest in Middlesborough. http://localhost:1313/blog/community-food-forest/

item.title: Perennial veg
item.description:
Perennial vegetables are less work and more nutritious but it’s a big cultural shift to adopt new veg. Here’s a simple table of perennial vegetable equivalents to annual vegetables to help the process.

Perennial vegetable alternatives to annual vegetables, a reference to help make the transition to perennial veg. http://localhost:1313/perennial-veg/

item.title: Lynda’s Tayberry fedge
item.description:
A fedge (or dead hedge) can have many uses, one of which is as a support for scrambling soft fruit like the Tayberry.

At the end of my Introduction to Forest Gardening talk at Llechryd Gardening Club, the Club Secretary Lynda kindly offered some Tayberry bushes, suggesting that they would grow well on the south side of the dead hedge. http://localhost:1313/blog/lyndas-tayberry-fedge/

item.title: Recycled ever-expanding plant labels
item.description:
These nifty plant labels keep on expanding with a growing branch, and are easy to make from old plastic milk bottles.

I used to tie ordinary plants to tree branches with bits of baling twine. http://localhost:1313/blog/recycled-ever-expanding-plant-labels/

item.title: Ground cover
item.description:
A step-by-step guide to establishing a living ground cover: to protect the soil, create a habitat for wildlife and provide you with a harvest. Win, win, win.

A key principle of no-dig gardening is to always protect the soil with mulch. http://localhost:1313/ground-cover/

item.title: How to establish ground cover in a forest garden
item.description:
A step-by-step guide to establishing a living ground cover: to protect the soil, create a habitat for wildlife and provide you with a harvest. Win, win, win.

A key principle of no-dig gardening is to always protect the soil with mulch. http://localhost:1313/blog/establish-ground-cover/

item.title: Forest garden plan
item.description:
This is the CAD plan for Forest Garden One

This is the latest version of the forest garden plan for what is unaffectionately known as Forest Garden One (I had a mental block with names that day). http://localhost:1313/blog/forest-garden-plan-pgi/

item.title: Ornamental Forest Garden plan
item.description:
This is the CAD plan for the Ornamental Forest Garden

The Ornamental Forest Garden (OFG) plan is based on a satellite photo, so it should be more accurate than the Forest Garden One, which is based on a digital Ordnance Survey map. http://localhost:1313/blog/forest-garden-plan-ofg/

item.title: Dead hedge
item.description:
A dead hedge, made from posts and scrubby branches, is an ideal temporary windbreak and wildlife habitat.

The benefits of a dead hedge, made from cut branches and foliage are: http://localhost:1313/dead-hedge/

item.title: DBIF
item.description:
An invaluable tool in the wildlife gardeners’ toolbox, The Database of Insects and their Food Plants details scientifically corroborated interactions between insects and plants

The Database of Insects and their Food Plants (DBIF for short) is a fantastic resource for wildlife gardeners. http://localhost:1313/dbif/

item.title: Emergency
item.description:
A forest garden works with nature to grow edible crops, which is why it’s good for combatting Climate Emergency and Mass Extinction. Here are 11 reasons why.

The Climate Emergency and Mass Extinction is caused by living in an unsustainable way. http://localhost:1313/emergency/

item.title: Why a forest garden is good for climate change
item.description:
A forest garden works with nature to grow edible crops, which is why it’s good for climate change. Here are the reasons why.

Climate change is caused by living in an unsustainable way. Forest gardens by definition are a sustainable and sustaining form of gardening. http://localhost:1313/blog/forest-garden-good-for-climate-change/

item.title: Forest garden itinerary
item.description:
The itinerary for my forest garden tours and workshops, with a link to a “live” page that will be kept up-to-date.

We had our first forest garden tour and workshop last week, via Airbnb. http://localhost:1313/blog/forest-garden-itinerary/

item.title: Creating a simple propagation bed
item.description:
Now is always a good time to start propagating ground cover plants for a forest garden, indeed any garden. Here’s how to make a simple, no-dig propagation bed, where you can plant-and-forget.

At the beginning of any forest garden project, I always recommend surveying and planning first, followed by propagating ground cover plants. http://localhost:1313/blog/howto-propagation-bed/

item.title: Grow your own website
item.description:
Advice on how to set up a website for an organisation, as promised to Teresa Filipponi at the Hub Cymru Africa permaculture event at Botanic Garden Wales!

Jumping on the website and social media bandwagon is difficult if you don’t have the technical experience or the budget. http://localhost:1313/blog/grow-your-own-website/

item.title: Easy graft
item.description:
Both of my apple grafts from The People’s Orchard workshop have taken! Beginners luck methinks…

A couple of months ago, I went on a grafting workshop run by Steve Wilson and organised by The People’s Orchard. http://localhost:1313/blog/easy-graft/

item.title: Splashes of colour in a sea of shimmering green
item.description:
These are some of my favourite flowers, simple but colourful flowers, adding a splash of nectar, pollen & colour to that verdant forest garden green

I like forest gardens, obviously. Although they can be very… green. http://localhost:1313/blog/splashes-colour-sea-shimmering-green/

item.title: Gardening in the vernacular
item.description:
Forest gardening is not a “style”, rather it is a practical and considerate way of gardening, and its beauty is drawn from necessity. It is the ultimate in vernacular gardening.

Long before I stumbled into forest gardening, I always loved allotments. http://localhost:1313/blog/gardening-vernacular/

item.title: Bird nest box plan
item.description:
There’s still time to make a bird nesting box for your garden. All you need is 4½ feet of 6x1, a saw, half a dozen screws, a bit of old inner tube and a hole saw.

The whole point of a forest garden is that you are working with nature to grow edible crops. http://localhost:1313/blog/bird-nest-box-plan/

item.title: Nest box
item.description:
Make a bird nesting box for your garden. All you need is 4½ feet of 6"x1" timber, a saw, half a dozen screws, a bit of old inner tube and a hole saw.

The whole point of a forest garden is that you are working with nature to grow edible crops. http://localhost:1313/nest-box/

item.title: Spacing
item.description:
The gaps between tree canopies should be about ¼ to ½ of the average tree canopy diameter. This is to allow enough light to reach the understorey plants.

It’s important to leave enough space between trees in the canopy layer, so that there’s enough light coming through to the plants below. http://localhost:1313/spacing/

item.title: Tree spacing in the forest garden
item.description:
The gaps between tree canopies should be about ¼ to ½ of the average tree canopy diameter. This is to allow enough light to reach the understorey plants.

It’s important to leave enough space between trees in the canopy layer, so that there’s enough light coming through to the plants below. http://localhost:1313/blog/tree-spacing-forest-garden/

item.title: Wildflower meadow genesis
item.description:
A daily rambling about the plan for a wildflower meadow at Forest Garden Wales.

A neighbour has very kindly lent us a brush cutter, so I’ve been busy for the past couple of weeks clearing the bramble from the coppice area (pictured above) to plant more trees, and the 2 acre sloping pasture so that we can create a wildflower meadow. http://localhost:1313/blog/wildflower-meadow-genesis/

item.title: Act right now on climate
item.description:
In a rekindling of my daily weblog format, here’s some thoughts about some really important things.

I went on the local Strike4Climate demonstration with my two children on Friday, part of their education in our democratic ways. http://localhost:1313/blog/weblog-act-right-now-on-climate/

item.title: Designing a small forest garden
item.description:
A small garden can be a forest garden, and this is a peek into how I approach a small forest garden design.

I really think that your small garden can be a forest garden, because to be a forest garden is more abut the principles than the size. http://localhost:1313/blog/designing-small-forest-garden/

item.title: Flowers for pollinators in an orchard
item.description:
Orchards need pollinators, which in turn need particular help in spring. Based on DNA barcoding research by @WalesBotanic, these are the top spring flowers. This article first appeared in The People’s Orchard Newsletter.

Steve Wilson, the tutor at a recent People’s Orchard grafting workshop, discussed how his wildflower meadow encourages pollinators. http://localhost:1313/blog/flowers-pollinators-orchard/

item.title: Our forest garden tours & workshops setup
item.description:
The majority of Forest Garden Wales workshops & tours are on Airbnb, with monthly discount slots on Facebook/Eventbrite.

I’ve organised the forest garden tours and workshops for 2019. I decided to market them as Airbnb holiday Experiences because it is a vast amount of work to manage an online shop, calendar and publicity. http://localhost:1313/blog/forest-garden-tours-workshops-setup/

item.title: Leave space
item.description:
The first stage of the Ornamental Forest Garden is complete and there are large gaps about the place. This is fine, for now.

We’ve planted the canopy layer in the Ornamental Forest Garden, a new, greenfield, 1 acre site. http://localhost:1313/blog/leave-space/

item.title: Most attractive fruit colours to birds
item.description:
Reading about the Kordia sweet black cherry, I remembered my notes saying black was the least favourite fruit colour of birds

In descending order of attractiveness: Purple Red Orange Black (mulberries) Not sure where Martin Crawford sourced this information from. http://localhost:1313/blog/attractive-fruit-colour/

item.title: Condensed forest garden knowledge
item.description:
You have 3 minutes to advise someone starting a forest garden—what are the most important things?

I happened to be passing Farmyard Nurseries (where I accidentally bought some Mentha longifolia) and met someone who wants to start a forest garden. http://localhost:1313/blog/condensed-forest-garden-knowledge/

item.title: Layers
item.description:
A quick reference for the seven layers of a forest garden, because I can only ever remember 3 of them (see my logo)…

@AgroforestryT has a definition of forest gardening and helpfully lists the “seven layers” of a forest garden: http://localhost:1313/layers/

item.title: The seven layers of a forest garden
item.description:
A quick reference for the multiple layers of a forest garden, because I can only ever remember 3 of them (see my logo)…

@AgroforestryT has a definition of forest gardening and helpfully lists the “seven layers” of a forest garden: http://localhost:1313/blog/seven-layers-forest-garden/

item.title: How to plant & mulch a weedy windbreak
item.description:
We’ve been planting an 80 metre long, 5 metre high windbreak on the northern boundary of the Ornamental Forest Garden. If you’re doing something similar, you may find these notes handy.

We’ve planted Amelanchier canadensis, Elaeagnus umbellata, Berberis darwinii and Viburnum opulus in the northern double-width windbreak hedge of the Ornamental Forest Garden. http://localhost:1313/blog/plant-mulch-windbreak/

item.title: Windbreak
item.description:
Protection is critical in the forest garden and windbreaks provide habitat and food as well as protection from the wind.

Windbreaks are essential for protecting plants in the forest garden. There is always a balance to be struck between protection and sunlight. http://localhost:1313/windbreak/

item.title: The joy of forest garden planning
item.description:
It’s not glamorous, it’s not plants but I do really enjoy the design and planning stages of a forest garden. It’s the best time to make the big mistakes 😉

Things have started happening in the Ornamental Forest Garden—we have the beginnings of a Northern Hedge, the plants from ScotPlants Direct are arriving tomorrow, and the digger cometh. http://localhost:1313/blog/joy-of-planning/

item.title: Forest garden thug or doer?
item.description:
What is the difference between a plant which is a good performer and one which is an aggressive thug?

Some plants in the garden if left to their own devices will just take over, bramble (Rubus fruticosus) being an excellent example. http://localhost:1313/blog/thug-or-doer/

item.title: The People’s Orchard Grafting Workshop with Steve Wilson
item.description:
These are my rough and ready notes from the grafting workshop for my personal consumption.

Discussion about pollinator friendly flowers. Botanic Garden Wales research on top flowers for pollination, link to blurry slide from slideshow by @abigailjayne26. http://localhost:1313/blog/grafting-workshop-feb-2019/

item.title: Benefits of a Forest Garden
item.description:
These are just some of the benefits of a forest garden

Plentiful, reliable and low maintenance food crops Biodiversity, shelter and increased wildlife Low energy, low input gardening Improved soil health and vitality Captures carbon via trees and soil life Beautiful & functional http://localhost:1313/blog/benefits-forest-garden/

item.title: Windbreak hedges for an exposed coastal site
item.description:
A chance encounter has me thinking about optimal plants for a windbreak hedge that will deal with salt-laden gales

At last Saturday’s pruning workshop by The People’s Orchard I was talking with some people from Fishguard. http://localhost:1313/blog/windbreak-hedges-exposed-coastal-site/

item.title: Forest Garden Network
item.description:
We’ve just been added to this informal network of forest gardens, the aim of which is to facilitate visits between members.

The Forest Garden Network is a free and informal network of forest gardeners and agroforestry practitioners, administered by Martin Crawford from the Agroforestry Research Trust. http://localhost:1313/blog/forest-garden-network/

item.title: I know nothing
item.description:
After a pruning workshop with orchardist Martin Hayes, I marvel at the skills and knowledge of professional horticulturalists.

The People’s Orchard hosted a fantastically well attended pruning workshop on Saturday 26th January (about 27 people). http://localhost:1313/blog/i-know-nothing/

item.title: Three ways to describe a forest garden
item.description:
I worked with a computer technician called Three-Way George. If you asked him how to do something, he’d always reply “Well, there are three ways…”. Likewise, there are (at least) three ways to describe a forest garden.

It’s always handy to have some simple forest garden definitions to hand. http://localhost:1313/blog/three-ways/

item.title: The place you want to be
item.description:
Garden designer Sarah Price gave an insightful and motivating talk at Botanic Garden Wales ‘Inspirational Women in Horticulture’ series. It piqued my curiosity about the role of aesthetics in gardening, and the wider, age-old framing of form and function.

Sarah Price gave a magnificent talk for the Inspirational Women in Horticulture series, tracing her professional career as a garden designer, whilst simultaneously stimulating key questions about what and how a garden is and does. http://localhost:1313/blog/the-place-you-want-to-be/

item.title: The forest garden in midwinter
item.description:
A small garden web log about the middle of winter in the forest garden

Looking forward to the spring, I’m running our first forest garden tours and workshops. http://localhost:1313/blog/forest-garden-midwinter-2019/

item.title: Your small garden can be a forest garden
item.description:
The term “forest garden” can be misleading. It’s neither forest-sized nor a forest, so rest assured that your small garden can be transformed into a forest garden.

One drawback with forest gardening is the name. Firstly, it emulates the edge of a woodland, not the deepest, darkest depths of a forest. http://localhost:1313/blog/small-forest-garden/

item.title: Inspirational women (some of them in horticulture)
item.description:
Botanic Garden of Wales’ has an excellent series of talks called Inspirational Women in Horticulture, I’ve drawn up my own list of inspirational women, some of whom are in horticulture.

I’m a latecomer to the gardening party and I sometimes feel like a gatecrasher, inadvertently swept in on the coat-tails of forest gardening (you know, the scruffy one with the wild look in its eye). http://localhost:1313/blog/inspirational-women-horticulture/

item.title: Now taking bookings for forest garden tours and workshops
item.description:
I’m very excited to announce that the 2019 Forest Garden Wales tours and workshops, known as “Experiences” in Airbnb speak, are now available to book!

Our 2019 workshops and tours are now available to book on Airbnb: http://localhost:1313/blog/forest-garden-experience/

item.title: Creating a forest garden in 8 easy steps
item.description:
Eight flavourful steps to tempt you into the exhilirating world of forest gardening. Creating a forest garden isn’t too difficult, it just takes hard work and perseverance. Like any garden.

We started creating a forest garden about 3 years ago. I always recommend people thoroughly read Creating a Forest Garden by Martin Crawford. http://localhost:1313/blog/creating-forest-garden-8-steps/

item.title: Gardening like a child
item.description:
When gardening with a 3 year old, you really have to let go and go with the flow.

I had planned to put up some chicken wire on the bottom of the gates to stop @ForestGdnDog from running out in the road. http://localhost:1313/blog/gardening-like-child/

item.title: The forest garden: it’s about time
item.description:
It’s about time forest gardening reached a wider audience. Nevertheless, a garden is also about time; a seasonal, cyclical time. Herein lies some temporal whimsy…

It started with a tweet by @AmielWayne : Wayne's original tweet back in December 2018 I was inspired to make a forest garden calendar, although rather predictably this hasn’t happened in time for 2019. http://localhost:1313/blog/forest-garden-about-time/

item.title: Cream-crackered lime
item.description:
In a sweltering 12°C of this mildest of winters, I’ve started spreading powdered lime in the Ornamental Forest Garden. Phew.

There’s a metric tonne of powdered lime in the Dutch Barn that needs to make way for the annual thinning of the 2 acre woodland. http://localhost:1313/blog/cream-crackered-lime/

item.title: Mulching raised beds
item.description:
Reinvigorated by Ruth Stout’s ‘No Work Garden Book’, I’m back to mulching all the raised beds.

Someone on @GdnBloggers recommended Ruth Stout’s No Work Garden Book, and it has been a true delight, full of little mulching nuggets of practical wisdom. http://localhost:1313/blog/mulching-raised-beds/

item.title: Gardening with urgency and purpose
item.description:
The purpose within a forest garden is to create a beautiful and productive space which meets the myriad challenges of climate change.

I am not a natural born gardener. I’ve come to it late, initially provoked by the threat of climate change and tantalised by the prospect of a productive, low maintenance, wildlife friendly forest garden. http://localhost:1313/blog/gardening-with-urgency-purpose/

item.title: Gardener’s log, start date 2018-12-20
item.description:
I thought I might start using the blog as a log of what I do in the garden, a diary of ramshackle thoughts, minor ramblings and garden ambles.

I’ve been inspired by Alison Levey @papaver’s blog the blackberry garden. http://localhost:1313/blog/gardeners-log/

item.title: Deturfing the Greenery
item.description:
After a couple of hours deturfing, I now remember why I prefer sheet mulching in order to remove grass.

Last week I decided to grow more greens in a dedicated Greenery area. http://localhost:1313/blog/deturf-greenery/

item.title: Sea Buckthorn planted in Willow Walk
item.description:
Three years after buying Hippophae rhamnoides 'Orange Energy', they’re finally planted in the forest garden Willow Walk.

I bought two Hippophae rhamnoides (Sea Buckthorn) ‘Orange Energy’ from Agroforestry Research Trust. http://localhost:1313/blog/sea-buckthorn-willow-walk/

item.title: The Groundcherry Experiment
item.description:
I nearly composted the whole Groundcherry bed and I’m very glad I didn’t.

This is the first year we grew Groundcherries AKA Cape Gooseberries AKA Physalis peruviana. http://localhost:1313/blog/groundcherry-experiment/

item.title: Where to store your garden blog photos
item.description:
One for you gdnbloggers – after a recent fracas with Flickr, a quick of summary of where online you can catalogue, store & link to your garden photos

Back in the day, I used WordPress to write my blog and moreover used it to upload all my worthy garden photos. http://localhost:1313/blog/store-garden-blog-photos/

item.title: Eat your greens from the Forest Garden Greenery!
item.description:
I have neglected my greens in the forest garden, so I’m making them a Greenery, a place for perennial alternatives to cabbage and spinach.

My first piece of gardening advice is to grow something beautiful & edible near your house. http://localhost:1313/blog/eat-your-greens-forest-garden-greenery/

item.title: Now offering remote forest garden design service!
item.description:
Very happy to announce I’m now offering a remote forest garden design service. Using email and Skype, I can create an outline forest garden plan for only £100.

“I’m just delighted with the plan! It’s everything I wanted and much more. http://localhost:1313/blog/remote-forest-garden-design/

item.title: ‘Miniature Woodlands’ at Chelsea Flower Show
item.description:
Thankfully there is a shift in traditional gardening toward trees & wildlife.

Very interesting news article in the @Telegraph by Helena Horton @horton_official, entitled ‘Miniature woodlands’ which help beat pollution set to overshadow traditional gardens at Chelsea Flower Show. http://localhost:1313/blog/miniature-woodlands-chelsea-flower-show/

item.title: My first ever paid forest garden design job
item.description:
Coloured Poundland pencils come to the rescue in my outline design for a small forest garden.

I’m proud to have finished my first paid forest garden design. http://localhost:1313/blog/first-forest-garden-design-job/

item.title: Now is the best time for hardwood cuttings
item.description:
A gentle reminder to myself & others to propagate large quantities of hardwood cuttings – they’re so simple & quick, and the rewards are bountiful.

The best time for hardwood cuttings is from mid-autumn until late winter. http://localhost:1313/blog/when-is-the-best-time-for-hardwood-cuttings/

item.title: The surveying legwork of the Ornamental #ForestGarden
item.description:
Oh, if only I could start planting the Ornamental Forest Garden straight away. But no. I need to survey the land before I make my Cunning Plan, before I get to the joy of planting.

“Never mind surveying the forest garden plot, my plants are arriving this afternoon! http://localhost:1313/blog/surveying-ornamental-forest-garden/

item.title: Introducing the OFG: Ornamental Forest Garden!
item.description:
I’m on a mission to prove that a forest garden can be as aesthetically beautiful as it is productive, wildlife friendly & low maintenance.

Yes, you can have it all, beauty and utility. Well, this is what I’m hoping to prove with the Ornamental Forest Garden. http://localhost:1313/blog/introducing-ornamental-maintenance-free-forest-garden/

item.title: My social media blog experiment
item.description:
Is bulk auto-scheduling akin to spam, or is it a useful tool for promoting your blog?

I have a love/hate relationship with social media. On the one hand, it has opened up completely new gardening and wildlife worlds, via groups such as #GdnBloggers. http://localhost:1313/blog/social-media-blog-experiment/

item.title: Benefits of a forest garden
item.description:
TL;DR low maintenance, productive, biodiversity, resilient 🙂

Forest gardening is a productive, low maintenance, wildlife friendly way to garden. http://localhost:1313/blog/benefits-of-a-forest-garden/

item.title: Top 10 bee friendly plants for the forest garden
item.description:
Bring a splash of colour to your forest garden with some stalwart “system plants”, providing a much needed boost of all-year-round nectar and pollen for your friendly pollinators and predators 🐝

The whole idea behind a forest garden is that it works with nature, to be as self-sustaining as possible. http://localhost:1313/blog/top-10-bee-friendly-plants/

item.title: Weeding forest garden style
item.description:
The “chop ’n‘ drop” method of mulching applies equally to weeding in the forest garden

In the chapter on Maintenance in Creating a Forest Garden, Martin Crawford says http://localhost:1313/blog/weeding-forest-garden-style/

item.title: A forest garden is a way to garden
item.description:
After listening to a @margaretroach podcast & reading a @DaveGoulson book, I realised that I don’t need to worry about plants!

I listened to the Margaret Roach podcast A Way to Garden, the episode entitled “Go ahead, we dare you: widen your plant palette”, with Andy Brand. http://localhost:1313/blog/forest-garden-is-way-to-garden/

item.title: A Tale of Three Trees
item.description:
Three Italian Alder, three very different outcomes

Three years ago I planted 3 Alnus cordata (Italian Alder) along the top of the forest garden, all at the same time. http://localhost:1313/blog/a-tale-of-three-trees/

item.title: When you are dying, surround yourself with life
item.description:
@doctor_oxford’s article on palliative care and nature struck a chord with my own experiences

Dr Rachel Clarke @doctor_oxford, palliative care doctor and author, has written a beautiful piece in the New York Times called In Life’s Last Moments, Open a Window. http://localhost:1313/blog/when-you-are-dying/

item.title: I’ve had enough, it’s time to quit!
item.description:
Sometimes, failure is the best option

For the past 3 years I’ve been leading a double life. http://localhost:1313/blog/time-to-quit/

item.title: Nettle soup
item.description:
Stalwart recipe of the foraging community

Ingredients ½ carrier bag stinging nettle tops 50g vegan margarine 1 large onion 1 litre vegetable stock 1 large potato, peeled & cut into cubes 1 large carrot, peeled & chopped Salt & pepper 2 tbsp Oatly creme fraiche Method Wash nettles, get rid of thick stalks, drain Melt vegan margarine in large saucepan, add onion, cook gently 5-7 minutes until softened Add stock, nettles, potato & carrot. http://localhost:1313/blog/nettle-soup/

item.title: The Lure of the Stinging-less Nettle
item.description:
A long time ago I heard about the legend of The Stinging-less Netlle, a fabled plant with all of the nitrogen-rich, low-maintenance and nutritional benefits of the stinging nettle without any of the stinging.

The stinging nettle Urtica dioica is an excellent forest garden plant. http://localhost:1313/blog/the-lure-of-the-stinging-less-nettle/

item.title: Vegan banana cake
item.description:
Very easy vegan banana cake, which I topped with our first ever gooseberries (Hinnomaki Red)!

We picked our first ever gooseberries last week. I washed them, popped them into a saucepan with a tiny bit of water, cooked them on a low heat for a few minutes until soft then added some sugar. http://localhost:1313/blog/vegan-banana-cake/

item.title: Definition
item.description:
A self-nourishing wildlife orchard underplanted with edible shrubs and perennial vegetables. Productive, sustainable and low-maintenance 💚 🌳

A forest garden mimics the edge of woodland and works with nature to grow produce sustainably for less work. http://localhost:1313/definition/

item.title: What is a Forest Garden?
item.description:
A forest garden is like a wildlife orchard underplanted with edible shrubs and perennial vegetables. It is productive, sustainable and low-maintenance 💚 🌳

With a forest garden, you can grow food sustainably for less work and more wildlife. http://localhost:1313/blog/what-is-a-forest-garden/

item.title: Vegan Chocolate Brownies
item.description:
A recipe for the kind of world famous cakes you can expect on our #ForestGarden tour!

Ingredients 250g plain flour 350g demerara sugar 65g plain cocoa powder 1 tsp baking powder 1 tsp salt 250ml water 250ml vegetable oil 1 tsp vanilla extract Method Preheat the oven to 180°C In a large bowl, stir together the flour, sugar, cocoa powder, baking powder and salt. http://localhost:1313/blog/vegan-chocolate-brownies/

item.title: Photographs from the Permaculture Tywi forest garden tour
item.description:
Photographs of the @permatywi visit to #ForestGarden Wales, by Christine Jones

Permaculture Tywi, a permaculture group from the Tywi Valley in Wales, came on a magnificent forest garden tour on Saturday 23rd June, as previously reported. http://localhost:1313/blog/forest-garden-tour-permaculture-tywi-photos/

item.title: Permaculture Tywi forest garden tour
item.description:
@PermaTywi came for a #ForestGarden tour on Saturday, about a dozen, everyone seemed to enjoy themselves!

Permaculture Tywi from Llandeilo way (the Tywi Valley) came for a #ForestGarden tour last Saturday. http://localhost:1313/blog/permaculture-tywi-tour/

item.title: 7 Magical Things about Forest Gardens
item.description:
With @GWandShows highlighting the benefits of #meadows, rain gardens & #plasticfree, have a look at the magic a #ForestGarden can provide 🙂

I gave a talk recently to a local gardening club and I wanted to highlight the benefits of a forest garden in an accessible way (if you didn’t know, a forest garden is a productive way to garden using trees, shrubs and perennial plants). http://localhost:1313/blog/7-magical-things/

item.title: The Green Tangle
item.description:
All that doesn’t glitter isn’t not gold! Or, it’s okay to have a green garden like a forest garden

As the saying goes, “You can have a show garden or you can have a real garden”. http://localhost:1313/blog/the-green-tangle/

item.title: How green is your garden?
item.description:
Gardening should be the most environmentally friendly of endeavours but that isn’t necessarily the case

I came across a post about how to Capture carbon in your garden, by @ModernMintLtd. http://localhost:1313/blog/how-green-is-your-garden/

item.title: Forest garden tours now public!
item.description:
Forest Garden Wales is now open for tours by individual members of the public. Tickets on Eventbrite 🙂

We’ve taken the plunge and opened up the forest garden tours for individual members of the public to come along, rather than just taking group bookings. http://localhost:1313/blog/forest-garden-tours-now-public/

item.title: My blue hay mulching experiment
item.description:
Grass in Welsh is glaswellt, which translates as “blue hay”. I’m using freshly scythed grass as a mulch in the polytunnel 🙂

Last year I experimented with a green mulch in the polytunnel. http://localhost:1313/blog/my-bluegrass-mulching-experiment/

item.title: Does woodchip mulch rob nitrogen from the soil?
item.description:
Many people believe that woodchip applied as a mulch around plants can tie-up nitrogen and cause deficiencies. Apparently not!

I’m a big fan of Martin Crawford and all he has done to promote the understanding and practise of forest gardening. http://localhost:1313/blog/does-woodchip-mulch-rob-nitrogen-from-the-soil/

item.title: Now using Netlify CMS!
item.description:
A bit of a geeky post about the software that powers forestgarden.wales

With my web designer/developer hat on, I built the Forest Garden Wales website using an amazing static site generator called Hugo, with my own socially-aware starter theme called Indiego. http://localhost:1313/blog/now-using-netlify-cms/

item.title: Heated propagator howto
item.description:
Instructions for making your own thermostatically controlled propagator, cribbed from @GWandShows

http://www.bbc.co.uk/gardenersworld/video/toby_30minfix/heated_propagator.shtml Equipment needed: Screwdriver Drill 13mm wood drill bit Knife Pliers 6m long heating cable and compatible thermostatic control Screws to hold the sides together and attach to base RCD device to plug into wall socket if your fuse board doesn’t already contain one. http://localhost:1313/blog/heated-propagator-howto/

item.title: Forest garden tours now operating!
item.description:
Thanks to inspriation from The People’s Orchard and Permaculture Tywi, we now offer a half-day forest garden tour and talk! £10 per person, minimum 5 people

After the incredibly positive and encouraging feedback from The People’s Orchard and Permaculture Tywi, we’ve decided to offer a half-day tour and talk of our forest garden in Ceredigion. http://localhost:1313/blog/tours-now-operating/

item.title: The Order of Things
item.description:
“Critical” was my word of the day on a recent tour, so I thought I’d formalise the list of things I think are critical when creating a forest garden

Plan Windbreaks Propagation Canopy Mulch Ground cover Rabbit fence To be honest, this probably applies equally to all gardens! http://localhost:1313/blog/the-order-of-things/

item.title: The People’s Orchard visit Forest Garden Wales
item.description:
A legion of People Orchardoers made the inaugural tour around Forest Garden Wales

After giving my first ever forest garden talk to The People’s Orchard back in March, Nia asked if they could have tour of Forest Garden Wales own forest garden. http://localhost:1313/blog/the-peoples-orchard-visit-forest-garden-wales/

item.title: The promise of planting a tree
item.description:
Fun but serious work on Thursday morning, planting an apple and plum orchard by the car park at Poppit Sands, St Dogmaels, Pembrokeshire

There is nothing like the promise of planting a tree. On an overcast Thursday morning in mid-winter late April, Nia and Sophie from The People’s Orchard, with experienced orchardist Martin Hayes, led a small band of resilient volunteers in planting apple and plum trees by the car park at Poppit Sands in Pembrokeshire. http://localhost:1313/blog/the-promise-of-planting-a-tree/

item.title: What blogging software do you use?
item.description:
After days of building my own blog, I’m curious as to what other people are using and how well it fits in with social media.

My alter ego is a web designer. This is a blessing and a curse, for I have spent too long building the new Forest Garden Wales website blog and not enought time gardening. http://localhost:1313/blog/what-blogging-software-do-you-use/

item.title: Notes from Wild Bees in Pembrokeshire talk
item.description:
Wild Bees in Pembrokeshire - an introduction to Species, Diversity and Learning to Record Wildlife with Clare Flynn @wildaboutnature, talk at Moylegrove Old School Hall, Tue 23 Jan 7.30pm

Clare Flynn, County Recorder, @wildaboutnature Reasons for Biological Recording Reliable biological data Conservation - knowing what is there Monitoring populations Planning land use Provides data for academic research 3 paths: http://localhost:1313/blog/bee-notes/

item.title: Joy of Plants app for iPhone
item.description:
A quick heads up for handy plants database app

On a visit to Penrallt Garden Centre last year, I was trying to track down Mentha longifolia (horse mint) after seeing it used as a rampant ground cover on Martin Crawford’s forest gardening course. http://localhost:1313/blog/joy-of-plants/

item.title: My maiden forest garden talk!
item.description:
A 1 hour talk entitled ‘An introduction to forest gardening’, St Dogmaels Abbey, 7.30PM Friday 23rd February 2018, hosted by The People’s Orchard

The good people at The People’s Orchard are very kindly hosting my very first public talk about forest gardening. http://localhost:1313/blog/forest-garden-talk/

item.title: Plants that make mycorrhizal associations
item.description:
Excerpt from Radical Mycology listing which plants have which mycorrhizal associations

Edited notes from the Radical Mycology book by Peter McCoy. Handy knowledge when making your own mycorrhizal inoculant. http://localhost:1313/blog/list-mycorrhizal-associations/

item.title: How to make your own mycorrhizal fungi
item.description:
You can make your own arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi inoculant, like rootgrow™, for adding to roots for healthier plants. Notes & photos kindly from Antony & Emma of @HyphaeShroomery. Photos copyright @HyphaeShroomery.

Introduction Many gardeners add mycorrhizal fungi (like Chaos Fungorum or rootgrow™) to roots when planting. http://localhost:1313/blog/make-your-own-mychorrizal-inoculum/

item.title: Don’t stake fruit trees
item.description:
I am always wary of outright statements but this one is interesting and time saving

On a recent Planting & Pruning workshop held by The People’s Orchard, the orchardist Martin Hayes said a very interesting thing: don’t stake fruit trees unless they are dwarf ie if they are M26 or smaller. http://localhost:1313/blog/dont-stake-fruit-trees/

item.title: About the name…
item.description:
A small clarification about the origins of the name Forest Garden Wales

The name means “a forest garden, in Wales”. It does not mean “The Only Forest Garden in Wales”. http://localhost:1313/blog/about-the-name/

item.title: Forest Garden Design Course notes 2016
item.description:
Notes from Martin Crawford forest garden course back in the summer of 2016

Traditional agricultural science can’t cope with complexity of forest garden, 100+ species of plants. http://localhost:1313/blog/course-notes-2016/

item.title: The People’s Orchard pruning and planting workshop
item.description:
Notes from another excellent pruning and planting workshop with Martin Hayes, Orchardist, near Llandudoch in Pembrokeshire

The People’s Orchard put on another pruning and planting workshop today, over at Waun Segur farm in Llandudoch. http://localhost:1313/blog/the-peoples-orchard-pruning/

item.title: Blank canvas and the Prickly Quad
item.description:
The sheet mulch is up, the soil is bare and my mind is blank

The idea of a blank canvas fills me with an odd sense of dread. http://localhost:1313/blog/blank-canvas-and-the-prickly-quad/

item.title: Dwarf comfrey ground cover planted in Shrubbery
item.description:
Symphytum ibericum (dwarf or iberian comfrey) is a good mineral accumulator ground cover for shady areas, planted buckets of the stuff today

Amidst the driving wind and hail, I planted out about 100m² of Symphytum ibericum in the Shrubbery. http://localhost:1313/blog/dwarf-comfrey/

item.title: Growing the Coppice
item.description:
Starting the final section of the Coppice, with sweet chestnut to be planted soon

We’re very lucky to have the space to plant a coppice for our woodburners. http://localhost:1313/blog/growing-the-coppice/

item.title: Winter visit and forest garden plants at Colby Woodland Garden
item.description:
All manner of forest garden stalwarts spotted on a winter visit to the National Trust Colby Woodland Garden near Amroth in Pembrokeshire

Last weekend we paid a visit to Colby Woodland Garden in Pembrokeshire. http://localhost:1313/blog/colby-woodland-gardens/

item.title: Diversity in all things, including windbreaks
item.description:
A mixed species north boundary windbreak hedge has grown on leaps and bounds

Two years ago, we planted a 90 metre windbreak hedge along the north boundary of the forest garden with a mix of four species: http://localhost:1313/blog/diversity-in-all-things/

item.title: Even in the smallest moments
item.description:
No matter how much administration there is in the world, I try to find some time to garden

Today was filled with filling in a tax return, the UK deadline looming only days away. http://localhost:1313/blog/even-in-the-smallest-moments/

item.title: Tray cupboard from the dump!
item.description:
Picked up a freebie old chipboard cupboard from the dump, working fantastically as a tray cupboard. +1 recycling

After a family outing to the local dump, I managed to pick up an almost completely knackered cupboard. http://localhost:1313/blog/tray-cupboard/

item.title: Misty valley whilst releasing mouse
item.description:
Not exactly forest gardening but it was lovely and misty this morning in the hills where I released the mouse

Out on a walk to release the humanely caught mouse into the cold, cold woodland up the road, and the mist was all up and about the trees yet with blue sky above. http://localhost:1313/blog/misty-valley/

item.title: Unglamorous grunt work
item.description:
In a garden, it’s 95% perspiration and 5% inspiration

I’ve just done some calculations for our one acre forest garden. http://localhost:1313/blog/unglamorous-grunt-work/

item.title: Rootstocks
item.description:
Fruit varities are grafted on rootstocks, which determine the trees height and vigour. This is a quick reference for the different rootstocks available.

A roostock is the well developed root system on which you graft a known fruiting variety. http://localhost:1313/rootstocks/

item.title: Planting & Pruning workshop notes
item.description:
Scant notes from the Planting & Pruning workshop held by The People’s Orchard in St Dogmaels on 7th December 2017

On 7th December, I had the good fortune to attend a free Planting & Pruning workshop, held by The People’s Orchard based St Dogmaels Abbey. http://localhost:1313/blog/planting-pruning-workshop-notes/

item.title: Predator strip!
item.description:
Martin Crawford’s predator strip is a combination of perennial flowering plants to attract predatory and pollinating insects

On the longest night in the depths of winter, with temperatures plummeting to 10°C, the Brachyglottis (Dunedin Group) ‘Sunshine’ is flowering! http://localhost:1313/blog/predator-strip/

item.title: Welcome to Forest Garden Wales website!
item.description:
Forest Garden Wales has a website

Welcome to our new website, built by Grow Digital. Like a garden, there is always plenty to do and we will be rolling out our new design soon. http://localhost:1313/blog/welcome-to-forest-garden-wales-website/

item.title: Footpath widths
item.description: 0.6m — 1 adult only 0.9m — 2 adults just 0. http://localhost:1313/footpath-widths/
